title: NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(a) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.36: NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.36' running on Firewall and IDS logs tagged with zone, nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Firewall and IDS logs tagged with `zone`, `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv`
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809)
spl:
index=netfw earliest=-24h
| lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed
| where allowed="false"
| stats sum(bytes) as bytes count as sessions by src_zone dest_zone dest_port app
| where sessions > 10
| sort - bytes
| table src_zone, dest_zone, dest_port, app, bytes, sessions
description: Operational technology environments rely on strict segmentation between IT and OT. This use case detects east-west flows that violate Purdue zone boundaries — for example SMB or RDP from corporate VLANs into PLC subnets documented as prohibited.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes netfw. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Standardise zone naming across OT and IT firewalls; (2) encode allowed zone pairs in policy lookup; (3) alert SOC-OT fusion cell on any denied-pattern traffic that actually occurred (policy violation or mis-tagging); (4) weekly review with plant engineering; (5) document change tickets for approved exceptions. Indexes required: `index=netfw`.
visualization: Sankey (zone to zone), Table (violations), Single value (distinct forbidden paths), Heatmap (sessions by hour).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - agg_value
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Firewall and IDS logs tagged with `zone`, `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Standardise zone naming across OT and IT firewalls; (2) encode allowed zone pairs in policy lookup; (3) alert SOC-OT fusion cell on any denied-pattern traffic that actually occurred (policy violation or mis-tagging); (4) weekly review with plant engineering; (5) document change tickets for approved exceptions.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=netfw earliest=-24h
| lookup nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv src_zone dest_zone OUTPUT allowed
| where allowed="false"
| stats sum(bytes) as bytes count as sessions by src_zone dest_zone dest_port app
| where sessions > 10
| sort - bytes
| table src_zone, dest_zone, dest_port, app, bytes, sessions
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation (Art. 21(2)(a))** — Operational technology environments rely on strict segmentation between IT and OT. This use case detects east-west flows that violate Purdue zone boundaries — for example SMB or RDP from corporate VLANs into PLC subnets documented as prohibited.

Documented **Data sources**: Firewall and IDS logs tagged with `zone`, `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: netfw.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=netfw, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where allowed="false"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **src_zone dest_zone dest_port app**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Filters the current rows with `where sessions > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - agg_value
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**NIS2 OT Network Segmentation Validation (Art. 21(2)(a))** — Operational technology environments rely on strict segmentation between IT and OT. This use case detects east-west flows that violate Purdue zone boundaries — for example SMB or RDP from corporate VLANs into PLC subnets documented as prohibited.

Documented **Data sources**: Firewall and IDS logs tagged with `zone`, `nis2_ot_segment_policy.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: app, bytes, dest_port, dest_zone, sessions, src_zone.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sankey (zone to zone), Table (violations), Single value (distinct forbidden paths), Heatmap (sessions by hour).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for operational technology environments rely on strict segmentation between it and ot. this use case detects east-west flows that violate purdue zone boundaries — for example smb or rdp from corporate. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
